questions.text: Which of the following is a molecular ion?
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: Introduction to Fundamental Concepts of Chemistry

options.explanation: NH₄⁺ is the ammonium ion, formed by the protonation of ammonia (NH₃). It is not a molecular ion as it results from adding a proton (H⁺) rather than losing or gaining an electron.
H₃O⁺, or the hydronium ion, is formed when water (H₂O) gains a proton (H⁺). Like NH₄⁺, it is not a molecular ion because it is created through protonation rather than an electron loss.

A molecular ion is a charged species formed when a neutral molecule loses or gains an electron without breaking apart. In this case, NH₃⁺ is formed when an ammonia (NH₃) molecule loses one electron, making it a true molecular ion. Other ions, such as NH₄⁺ or H₃O⁺, are formed by protonation (adding H⁺), not by electron loss, and SO₄²⁻ is an anion formed by gaining electrons or losing protons. Therefore, they are not molecular ions.
SO₄²⁻, or the sulfate ion, is an anion formed by the loss of protons or gain of electrons. It is not a molecular ion because it does not derive from a neutral molecule losing or gaining an electron.

questions.explanation: A molecular ion is a charged species formed when a neutral molecule loses or gains an electron without breaking apart. In this case, NH₃⁺ is formed when an ammonia (NH₃) molecule loses one electron, making it a true molecular ion. Other ions, such as NH₄⁺ or H₃O⁺, are formed by protonation (adding H⁺), not by electron loss, and SO₄²⁻ is an anion formed by gaining electrons or losing protons. Therefore, they are not molecular ions.

questions.text: Which one is a monoisotopic element?
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: Introduction to Fundamental Concepts of Chemistry

options.explanation: Chlorine has two stable isotopes, chlorine-35 and chlorine-37, making it not a monoisotopic element.
Hydrogen is not a monoisotopic element as it has three isotopes: protium (¹H), deuterium (²H), and tritium (³H), with only protium being stable.
A monoisotopic element has only one stable, naturally occurring isotope. Fluorine is a prime example, as virtually all of its atoms found in nature are the isotope fluorine-19 (¹⁹F).
Cadmium has multiple stable isotopes, including cadmium-110, cadmium-111, cadmium-112, cadmium-113, cadmium-114, and cadmium-116, so it is not monoisotopic.

questions.explanation: A monoisotopic element has only one stable, naturally occurring isotope. Fluorine is a prime example, as virtually all of its atoms found in nature are the isotope fluorine-19 (¹⁹F).

questions.text: Atoms having the same mass number but different atomic numbers are called?

options.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element that have the same number of protons but different numbers of neutrons, resulting in different mass numbers. For example, Carbon-12 and Carbon-14 are isotopes of Carbon.
Isobars are atoms of different elements that share the same mass number (total protons + neutrons). They have different chemical properties because they have different numbers of protons.

Isotones are atoms of different elements that have the same number of neutrons but different numbers of protons and thus different mass numbers. For instance, Carbon-14 and Nitrogen-15 are isotones, as they both contain 8 neutrons.
Isomers are compounds that have the same molecular formula but different structural arrangements or spatial orientations of atoms. They are not defined by mass number or atomic number.
